question: What is the difference between JVM, JRE, and JDK?
quickAnswer: JDK is the full development kit (compiler + JRE). JRE is the runtime environment (JVM + standard libraries). JVM is the virtual machine that executes bytecode. JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM.
keyPoints:
JDK = JRE + javac + tools (jar, javadoc)
JRE = JVM + standard class libraries (rt.jar)
JVM = class loader + runtime data areas + execution engine
JVM is platform-specific; bytecode is platform-neutral

question: Describe the JVM architecture — what are its main components?
quickAnswer: JVM has a Class Loader subsystem, Runtime Data Areas (heap, stack, method area, PC registers, native method stack), an Execution Engine (interpreter + JIT compiler), and a Garbage Collector. The class loader loads .class files; the execution engine runs bytecode.
keyPoints:
Class Loader: Bootstrap → Extension → Application (delegation model)
Runtime Data Areas: Heap (shared), Stack (per-thread), Method Area/Metaspace, PC Register, Native Stack
Execution Engine: Interpreter for startup; JIT compiles hot code to native
Garbage Collector lives in heap management

question: What is the JIT compiler and why does it matter for performance?
quickAnswer: JIT (Just-In-Time) compiles frequently executed bytecode into native machine code at runtime. This eliminates interpretation overhead for hot paths. The JVM profiles which methods are 'hot' (invoked many times) and compiles those, giving performance close to native code after warm-up.
keyPoints:
Interpreter is slow; JIT converts hot bytecode to native code
Threshold: C1 (client) compiles at ~1500 invocations, C2 (server) at ~10000
Tiered compilation (Java 7+): C1 → C2 pipeline
Warm-up period: app starts slow, then accelerates

question: What is the difference between stack memory and heap memory in the JVM?
quickAnswer: Stack stores method frames, local variables, and references — one stack per thread, LIFO structure. Heap stores all object instances and class-level data — shared across threads. Stack is faster but limited in size (StackOverflowError); heap is larger but GC managed (OutOfMemoryError).
keyPoints:
Stack: per-thread, holds frames (local vars + operand stack + return address)
Heap: shared, holds new'd objects; subject to GC
Primitive variables stored on stack; objects always on heap
StackOverflowError = stack exhausted; OutOfMemoryError = heap exhausted

question: Walk through what happens from writing Java code to running it.
quickAnswer: 1. javac compiles .java → .class (bytecode). 2. JVM's class loader loads .class. 3. Bytecode verifier checks safety. 4. Interpreter or JIT executes bytecode. The 'write once, run anywhere' promise comes from the bytecode being platform-neutral; only the JVM is platform-specific.
keyPoints:
.java → javac → .class (bytecode, not machine code)
Class loader: load → link (verify/prepare/resolve) → initialize
Bytecode verifier: ensures no illegal memory access
Execution: interpreter first, JIT for hot methods

question: What does the class loader do, and what is the parent delegation model?
quickAnswer: Class loaders load .class files into the JVM. They follow parent delegation: a class loader asks its parent first; only loads the class itself if the parent can't find it. The hierarchy is Bootstrap → Extension (Platform) → Application. This prevents malicious classes from shadowing core Java classes.
keyPoints:
Bootstrap CL: loads core Java (java.lang, java.util) from rt.jar / jrt:/
Extension/Platform CL: loads JDK extensions
Application CL: loads classpath classes
Delegation prevents spoofing — you can't shadow java.lang.String

question: What is the difference between compile-time and runtime errors in Java?
quickAnswer: Compile-time errors are caught by javac (syntax errors, type mismatches, missing methods). Runtime errors occur during execution (NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, ClassCastException). Static analysis tools and generics help catch some runtime errors at compile time.
keyPoints:
Compile-time: syntax errors, incompatible types, missing returns
Runtime: NPE, ClassCastException, StackOverflowError
Checked exceptions: declared in signature — caught at compile time
Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException subclasses): not forced at compile time

question: What are the 8 primitive types in Java and their sizes?
quickAnswer: byte(1), short(2), int(4), long(8), float(4), double(8), char(2), boolean(1 bit, JVM-dependent). Unlike objects, primitives are stored by value on the stack and have no methods. Use wrapper classes (Integer, Double, etc.) when an object is needed.
keyPoints:
Only 8: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean
long literals need L suffix: 9_000_000_000L
float literals need f suffix: 3.14f
char is UTF-16, range 0–65535
boolean size is undefined by JLS (JVM-dependent)

question: What is the difference between == and .equals() in Java?
quickAnswer: == compares references (memory addresses) for objects, and values for primitives. .equals() compares logical content. For String literals, == may work due to string interning, but new String("a") == new String("a") is false. Always use .equals() to compare object content.
keyPoints:
== on primitives: value comparison
== on objects: reference (address) comparison
.equals() default (Object): same as ==; override to compare content
String pool: literals are interned, so same-content literals share reference
Common trap: Integer == Integer works only for -128 to 127 (cache)

question: What is integer overflow in Java and how does it behave?
quickAnswer: Java integers wrap silently on overflow — no exception. int max is 2^31-1 = 2,147,483,647; adding 1 wraps to Integer.MIN_VALUE (-2,147,483,648). Use long or BigInteger when values may exceed int range. Math.addExact() throws ArithmeticException on overflow.
keyPoints:
int: -2^31 to 2^31-1; wraps on overflow (no exception)
Use long for large values; BigInteger for arbitrary precision
Math.addExact(), Math.multiplyExact() throw on overflow
Common bug: looping i++ past Integer.MAX_VALUE creates infinite loop
codeExample: System.out.println(Integer.MAX_VALUE + 1); // -2147483648 (wraps)

question: What is type casting in Java and what is the difference between widening and narrowing?
quickAnswer: Widening conversion (byte→int→long→double) is automatic and safe — no data loss. Narrowing (double→int) is explicit (cast required) and may lose precision or truncate. Casting between unrelated types throws ClassCastException at runtime.
keyPoints:
Widening: implicit, no cast needed, no data loss
Narrowing: explicit cast required, may lose data (double 9.9 → int 9)
Primitive widening order: byte < short < int < long < float < double
Object casting: must be actual type at runtime or ClassCastException
codeExample:
double d = 9.99;
int i = (int) d; // i = 9, truncated

question: What is the difference between >> and >>> in Java?
quickAnswer: >> is arithmetic right shift — preserves the sign bit (fills with 1 for negative numbers). >>> is logical right shift — always fills with 0s. For positive numbers they behave identically. >>> is useful for treating a value as unsigned (e.g., computing mid = (lo + hi) >>> 1 safely).
keyPoints:
>> arithmetic shift: sign bit preserved (negative stays negative)
>>> logical shift: always pads with 0 bits
-8 >> 1 = -4; -8 >>> 1 = 2147483644
Safe mid calculation: (lo + hi) >>> 1 avoids overflow vs (lo + hi) / 2
codeExample:
System.out.println(-8 >> 1);   // -4
System.out.println(-8 >>> 1);  // 2147483644

question: What is short-circuit evaluation in Java logical operators?
quickAnswer: && and || use short-circuit evaluation: if the left operand determines the result, the right operand is not evaluated. obj != null && obj.doSomething() is safe because if obj is null, the right side is skipped. & and | always evaluate both sides (bitwise operators, no short-circuit).
keyPoints:
&& short-circuits on false left side
|| short-circuits on true left side
& and | evaluate both sides (no short-circuit)
Use && for null-safe guards: if(list != null && !list.isEmpty())
Side effects in right operand may not execute with &&/||

question: What is the difference between i++ and ++i?
quickAnswer: i++ is post-increment: returns the current value then increments. ++i is pre-increment: increments first then returns the new value. In standalone statements (just i++) they're identical. The difference matters in expressions: int x = i++ gives x the old value; int x = ++i gives x the new value.
keyPoints:
i++ (post): use value, then increment
++i (pre): increment, then use value
In for loops: both are equivalent (i++ preferred by convention)
Always the same final value; difference is the expression result
Not thread-safe — use AtomicInteger for concurrency

question: What is the difference between break and continue in loops?
quickAnswer: break exits the loop entirely and continues after the loop. continue skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next iteration check. Both support labeled versions for nested loops: break outerLoop exits the outer loop, continue outerLoop skips to the outer loop's next iteration.
keyPoints:
break: terminates the loop (or switch)
continue: skips current iteration, goes to next check
Labeled break/continue for nested loops: break outerLabel;
break can also exit switch statements
Avoid excessive use — prefer early returns in methods

question: How does a switch statement differ from a chain of if-else if statements?
quickAnswer: switch compiles to a jump table (O(1) dispatch) for dense int values, making it potentially faster than if-else chains (O(n)). switch requires a compile-time constant expression; supports int, char, String (Java 7+), and enums. Switch expressions (Java 14+) are more concise and can return a value with ->.
keyPoints:
switch: O(1) with jump table for integers (dense values)
if-else: O(n) sequential evaluation
switch supports: int/Integer, char, String, Enum — not long, float
Fall-through: case without break continues to next case
Switch expression (Java 14+): case x -> value; (no fall-through, can return)

question: What is the difference between an array and an ArrayList in Java?
quickAnswer: Arrays are fixed-size, can hold primitives, and have better performance (no boxing). ArrayList is dynamic (resizes automatically), holds only objects (autoboxes primitives), provides more utility methods (add, remove, contains), and implements List interface. Use arrays for fixed-size primitive data; ArrayList for dynamic collections.
keyPoints:
Array: fixed size, primitives OK, O(1) access, contiguous memory
ArrayList: dynamic (grows 1.5x on resize), objects only, more methods
ArrayList backed by an Object[] array internally
Conversion: Arrays.asList() → fixed-size list; new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(...)) → mutable
Performance: array access slightly faster; ArrayList has boxing overhead

question: How do you find duplicates in an array efficiently?
quickAnswer: Use a HashSet: iterate the array, try to add each element; if add() returns false, it's a duplicate. This is O(n) time and O(n) space. For integer arrays in range [0, n-1], use the array itself as a hash — negate visited indices (O(n) time, O(1) space).
keyPoints:
HashSet approach: O(n) time, O(n) space — general purpose
Sorting approach: O(n log n) — adjacent duplicates easy to spot
In-place marking: negate arr[arr[i]] if integers in [0, n-1]
Bitset approach: O(n/64) space for known range
Streams: Arrays.stream(arr).boxed().collect(groupingBy(..., counting()))

question: What is a 2D array in Java and how is it stored in memory?
quickAnswer: A Java 2D array (int[][]) is an array of arrays — each row is a separate heap object. Rows can have different lengths (jagged arrays). It's not stored as a contiguous block like C 2D arrays. Access is arr[row][col]; declaring int[3][4] creates 1 outer array + 3 inner arrays.
keyPoints:
int[][] arr = new int[3][4]: one array of 3 references + 3 arrays of 4 ints
Rows are separate heap objects — not contiguous
Jagged arrays allowed: arr[0] = new int[2]; arr[1] = new int[5];
Column-major access (arr[i][j] vs arr[j][i]) matters for cache performance
Arrays.deepToString() for printing 2D arrays

question: Why is String immutable in Java and what are the benefits?
quickAnswer: String is immutable because its char[] (or byte[]) field is final and not exposed. Benefits: thread safety (no synchronization needed), string pool (reuse via interning), safe as HashMap key (hashCode won't change), and security (class names, file paths can't be mutated after validation).
keyPoints:
Immutability = value never changes after creation
String pool (intern pool): same literal → same object
Thread-safe: multiple threads can read the same String safely
HashMap key safety: hash won't change mid-use
Concatenation creates new objects: use StringBuilder in loops

question: What is the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
quickAnswer: String is immutable; every operation creates a new object. StringBuilder is mutable and not thread-safe — use it in single-threaded code. StringBuffer is mutable and thread-safe (synchronized methods) — use when shared across threads, but it's slower than StringBuilder. In practice, StringBuilder is the go-to for building strings in loops.
keyPoints:
String: immutable, thread-safe, good for constants
StringBuilder: mutable, NOT thread-safe, fastest for local use
StringBuffer: mutable, thread-safe (synchronized), slower
Java 8 compile optimization: String + literal → StringBuilder in non-loop code
Capacity: StringBuilder default capacity 16; grows 2x+2 on overflow

question: How does the Java String pool (intern pool) work?
quickAnswer: String literals are automatically placed in the string pool (PermGen pre-Java 8, Metaspace/heap Java 8+). When you write String s = "hello", the JVM checks the pool first; if "hello" exists, it reuses the reference. String.intern() manually adds a string to the pool. new String("hello") always creates a new heap object bypassing the pool.
keyPoints:
String pool = set of interned strings, stored in heap (Java 8+)
Literals are automatically interned at class load time
new String("x"): creates new object on heap, NOT in pool
s.intern(): adds to pool if absent, returns pooled reference
Comparison: s1 == s2 is true only if both from pool for same value

question: How would you reverse a String in Java? What about checking if it's a palindrome?
quickAnswer: Reverse: new StringBuilder(str).reverse().toString(). Palindrome: compare str with its reverse, or use two-pointer approach (compare chars from both ends). Two-pointer is O(n) time O(1) space; StringBuilder reverse is O(n) time O(n) space.
keyPoints:
StringBuilder.reverse() is simplest
Two-pointer: i=0, j=len-1, compare while i<j, i++, j--
Case-insensitive palindrome: str.toLowerCase() first
Ignore spaces/punctuation: filter non-alphanumeric first
CodePoints for Unicode: use codePoints() for emoji-safe reversal

question: Is Java pass-by-value or pass-by-reference? Explain with an example.
quickAnswer: Java is always pass-by-value. For primitives, a copy of the value is passed. For objects, a copy of the reference (memory address) is passed — not the object itself. You can mutate the object's fields through the copied reference, but reassigning the parameter inside the method doesn't affect the caller's variable.
keyPoints:
Primitives: copy of value — modifications don't affect caller
Objects: copy of reference — can mutate fields, but not reassign caller's variable
Confusion: passing an object feels like pass-by-reference because mutations are visible
Test: swap(a, b) via method won't actually swap caller's variables
To swap: use a wrapper object or return an array
codeExample:
void modify(int x) { x = 99; } // caller unchanged
void addItem(List<String> list) { list.add("x"); } // caller's list IS modified
void reassign(List<String> list) { list = new ArrayList<>(); } // caller unchanged

question: What is recursion and what is a stack overflow? How do you prevent it?
quickAnswer: Recursion is when a method calls itself. Each call adds a new frame to the call stack. Stack overflow (StackOverflowError) occurs when the stack runs out — usually from missing or never-reached base case. Prevent it by ensuring a clear base case, or convert deep recursion to iterative using an explicit stack or tail recursion optimization (Java doesn't natively optimize tail calls).
keyPoints:
Every recursive call: new stack frame (local vars + return address)
Base case: the termination condition; missing it → infinite recursion
StackOverflowError: default JVM stack ~512KB–1MB depending on JVM
Tail recursion: Java doesn't optimize it; long chains still blow stack
Fix: iterative with explicit Stack<>, or trampolining pattern

question: Why does modifying an object inside a method affect the caller, but reassigning it doesn't?
quickAnswer: When you pass an object, the method receives a copy of the reference (pointer). Calling methods or modifying fields via that reference changes the same object — visible to the caller. But assigning the parameter to a new object just changes the local copy of the reference; the caller's variable still points to the original object.
keyPoints:
Object parameter = copy of address (reference value)
Mutation via reference: visible to caller (same object in heap)
Reassignment of parameter: changes local copy only, caller unaffected
Strings appear immutable by reference: str = "new" inside method → caller unchanged
To 'return' a new value: use method return value, not parameter mutation

question: What is stored on the stack vs the heap in Java?
quickAnswer: Stack holds method frames: local primitive variables, object references, and method call metadata. Each thread has its own stack. Heap holds all object instances (created with new) and arrays — shared across threads. When a method exits, its stack frame is instantly reclaimed. Heap memory is reclaimed by GC.
keyPoints:
Stack: primitives, references (not the objects), method frames — per-thread
Heap: all objects (new Foo()), all arrays — shared across threads
Instance variables (inside objects) live on the heap with the object
Stack allocation/deallocation: O(1) pointer move — very fast
Heap: requires GC — slower but much larger

question: What causes a memory leak in Java despite having a garbage collector?
quickAnswer: GC only collects objects with no live references. Memory leaks happen when you unintentionally hold references to objects you no longer need: static collections that grow indefinitely, event listeners not removed, inner classes holding outer class references, ThreadLocal variables not cleaned up, or caches without eviction.
keyPoints:
Static List/Map fields that keep growing (most common)
Event listeners: adding listeners without removing them
Non-static inner classes: hold implicit reference to outer class
ThreadLocal in thread pools: values survive thread reuse
Caches without size bounds or TTL (use WeakHashMap or Guava Cache)
Tool: VisualVM, Eclipse MAT, or Java Flight Recorder to detect

question: What is escape analysis in the JVM?
quickAnswer: Escape analysis determines if an object created inside a method can 'escape' to outside code (returned, stored in a field, passed to another thread). If an object doesn't escape, the JVM can allocate it on the stack instead of the heap (stack allocation), or eliminate the allocation entirely (scalar replacement), reducing GC pressure.
keyPoints:
JIT performs escape analysis at runtime
Stack allocation: non-escaping objects put on stack → freed instantly on method exit
Scalar replacement: object fields treated as separate stack variables (no object created)
Synchronization elision: locks on non-escaping objects can be removed
Enabled by default in JVM; can observe with -XX:+PrintEscapeAnalysis

question: What is the difference between a class and an object in Java?
quickAnswer: A class is a blueprint/template defining fields and methods. An object is an instance — a concrete allocation in memory created with new. A single class can have millions of instances. The class itself exists once in the method area; each object exists on the heap with its own copy of instance fields.
keyPoints:
Class: compile-time blueprint, loaded once per ClassLoader
Object: runtime instance, heap-allocated, has its own field state
static fields/methods: belong to class (shared by all instances)
Instance fields/methods: belong to each object
Class object: Class<T> metadata accessible via .getClass() or Foo.class

question: What is the difference between deep copy and shallow copy?
quickAnswer: Shallow copy copies the object's field values, but nested object references are shared — both copies point to the same inner objects. Deep copy recursively copies all nested objects so the two copies are completely independent. Object.clone() is a shallow copy by default; deep copy requires manual implementation or serialization trick.
keyPoints:
Shallow: copies references to nested objects — mutations in one affect the other
Deep: new instances of all nested objects — fully independent
Object.clone(): shallow by default; override for deep copy
Alternatives: copy constructor, serialization/deserialization, Jackson ObjectMapper
Record types (Java 16+): immutable, so no copy needed

question: What happens if you don't override equals() and hashCode() and use objects as HashMap keys?
quickAnswer: The default equals() checks reference equality (==), and hashCode() uses the object's identity hash. Two logically equal objects with different references will be treated as different keys. To use objects correctly as keys, override both: hashCode() must return the same value for objects where equals() is true, and equals() must be consistent.
keyPoints:
Default equals(): reference comparison (==)
Default hashCode(): memory address-based
Map/Set contract: if a.equals(b) → a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()
Violation: two 'equal' objects go to different buckets → can't find stored value
IntelliJ/Lombok can generate correct equals+hashCode pairs
Immutable fields only in hashCode() — mutable keys can lose entries

question: What is the difference between extends and implements in Java?
quickAnswer: extends is used for class-to-class or interface-to-interface inheritance — a class can extend only one class (single inheritance). implements is used when a class adopts an interface contract — a class can implement multiple interfaces. Java disallows multiple class inheritance to avoid the diamond problem.
keyPoints:
extends: class inherits from one class (or interface extends interface)
implements: class fulfills interface contract — multiple allowed
Single class inheritance prevents diamond problem
Interface default methods (Java 8+): can have implementation, causing diamond — resolved by overriding
Abstract class: can extend + implement; provides partial implementation

question: What is method overriding? What are the rules?
quickAnswer: Overriding provides a subclass-specific implementation of a method defined in the parent. Rules: same method signature (name + params), same or covariant return type, access modifier same or wider, checked exceptions same or narrower. Use @Override annotation — it catches signature mismatches at compile time.
keyPoints:
Same name + same parameter list (not just same name)
Return type: same OR covariant (subclass type) in Java 5+
Access: can widen (protected → public) but not narrow (public → private)
Checked exceptions: can declare fewer or narrower, not wider
@Override: compiler error if no matching parent method (catches typos)
final methods cannot be overridden

question: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?
quickAnswer: Overloading (compile-time polymorphism): same method name, different parameter types/count — resolved at compile time. Overriding (runtime polymorphism): subclass replaces parent's method with same signature — resolved at runtime via vtable dispatch. Overloading is in the same class (or superclass); overriding is across parent-child classes.
keyPoints:
Overloading: same class, different params, compile-time binding (static dispatch)
Overriding: parent-child, same signature, runtime binding (dynamic dispatch)
Overloading resolved by declared type; overriding resolved by actual type
Cannot overload by return type alone
Static methods: can't be overridden (hidden instead), resolved at compile time

question: What is runtime polymorphism (dynamic dispatch) in Java?
quickAnswer: When you call an overridden method on a reference variable, Java resolves which implementation to call based on the actual runtime type of the object, not the declared type of the reference. This is achieved via virtual method tables (vtable). It enables 'programming to an interface' — code that works for all subtypes.
keyPoints:
Declared type (compile-time): determines which methods are accessible
Actual type (runtime): determines which override is called
Vtable: per-class table of method pointers updated by overrides
Static, private, and final methods: NOT polymorphic (resolved at compile time)
Instanceof + casting is a code smell — prefer polymorphism instead
codeExample:
Animal a = new Dog();
a.speak(); // calls Dog.speak(), not Animal.speak()

question: What is the difference between compile-time and runtime polymorphism?
quickAnswer: Compile-time polymorphism (static binding) is method overloading — the compiler picks the right method based on argument types. Runtime polymorphism (dynamic binding) is method overriding — the JVM picks the right method at runtime based on the actual object type. Runtime polymorphism is the core OOP mechanism for extensibility.
keyPoints:
Compile-time (static): overloading — resolved by javac
Runtime (dynamic): overriding — resolved at runtime via vtable
Static methods are statically bound (resolved by reference type, not object type)
Fields are not polymorphic — always resolved by reference type
Abstract methods force subclasses to override (no default implementation)

question: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface in Java?
quickAnswer: Abstract class: can have state (fields), constructors, concrete methods, and abstract methods. A class can extend only one abstract class. Interface: all fields are public static final, all methods are public (default/static allowed since Java 8). A class can implement multiple interfaces. Use abstract class for 'is-a' with shared state; interface for 'can-do' capability.
keyPoints:
Abstract class: state + partial impl + constructors; single inheritance only
Interface: no state (constants only), pure API contract; multiple allowed
Java 8 interfaces: default and static methods added
Java 9 interfaces: private methods for code reuse in defaults
When to use: Template Method pattern → abstract class; Strategy/Plugin → interface

question: When would you choose an abstract class over an interface?
quickAnswer: Choose abstract class when: you need shared state (fields), you want a constructor, you have non-public methods to share, or you want to provide a default implementation using those shared fields (Template Method pattern). Choose interface when you want to define a capability that unrelated classes can implement.
keyPoints:
Abstract class: shared state, constructors, protected helpers, single hierarchy
Interface: capability contract, multiple inheritance, loose coupling
Template Method: abstract class fits perfectly (protected abstract steps)
Mixin behavior: interfaces fit (Serializable, Comparable, Runnable)
Modern Java: interfaces are preferred due to default methods; abstract class for framework base classes

question: What are the four access modifiers in Java and what do they control?
quickAnswer: private: visible only within the class. default (package-private, no keyword): visible within the same package. protected: visible to the package + subclasses. public: visible everywhere. Best practice: use the most restrictive access level that still works — typically make fields private and expose via getters/setters.
keyPoints:
private: class only — tightest
package-private (no modifier): package only
protected: package + subclasses (even in different package)
public: everywhere
Order (least to most): private < package < protected < public
Inner classes can access outer class private members

question: What is encapsulation and why is it important?
quickAnswer: Encapsulation bundles data (fields) and the methods that operate on it, hiding internal implementation details. It enforces invariants (e.g., age can't be negative), allows changing implementation without breaking callers, and reduces coupling. Achieved via private fields + public getters/setters or immutable classes.
keyPoints:
Hide internal state: private fields
Control access: getter/setter with validation logic
Change implementation freely: callers use API, not internals
Invariants: setter can reject invalid values
Immutability: no setters → strongest encapsulation (no state change possible)

question: What is type erasure in Java generics and what are its limitations?
quickAnswer: At compile time, generic type parameters are erased and replaced with Object (or the upper bound). So List<String> and List<Integer> are the same class at runtime — there's only one .class file. Limitations: can't use instanceof with generics, can't create arrays of generic types, can't catch generic exceptions, and can't use primitives as type parameters.
keyPoints:
Type erasure: generic info removed at compile time, replaced with bounds
List<String> and List<Integer>: same raw type at runtime (List)
Cannot do: new T[], obj instanceof List<String>, catch(MyException<T>)
Cannot use primitives: List<int> invalid — use List<Integer>
Reified generics: arrays ARE reified (String[] knows its type); generics are not
Heap pollution: unchecked casts can corrupt a generic collection

question: What is the difference between <? extends T> and <? super T> in Java generics?
quickAnswer: ? extends T (upper bound) means 'T or any subtype' — use for reading (producer). ? super T (lower bound) means 'T or any supertype' — use for writing (consumer). The PECS mnemonic: Producer Extends, Consumer Super. List<? extends Number> is read-only (you don't know the exact type); List<? super Integer> accepts Integer writes.
keyPoints:
PECS: Producer Extends, Consumer Super
? extends T: can read T; can't add (type unknown)
? super T: can add T; read returns Object
Collections.copy(dest, src): dest is List<? super T>, src is List<? extends T>
Unbounded <?>: read-only, accepts any type
Comparable<? super T>: common in sort methods to accept parent comparators

question: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?
quickAnswer: ArrayList: backed by array — O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete in the middle (shifts elements). LinkedList: doubly-linked list — O(n) random access, O(1) insert/delete at a known node. In practice ArrayList is faster for most use cases due to better cache locality. LinkedList is only faster for frequent insertions at both ends (use ArrayDeque instead).
keyPoints:
ArrayList: O(1) get, O(n) add/remove in middle, 1.5x resize
LinkedList: O(n) get, O(1) add/remove at ends — but needs pointer traversal
Cache locality: ArrayList contiguous memory → faster in practice
LinkedList: 2x memory overhead (prev/next pointers per node)
Prefer ArrayDeque over LinkedList for queue/deque operations

question: What is the difference between HashMap and TreeMap and LinkedHashMap?
quickAnswer: HashMap: unordered, O(1) average operations, null keys allowed. TreeMap: sorted by key (natural order or comparator), O(log n) operations, no null keys. LinkedHashMap: insertion-order maintained (or access-order for LRU), O(1) operations. Choose based on ordering needs.
keyPoints:
HashMap: hash table, unordered, O(1) avg, allows null key/value
TreeMap: Red-Black tree, sorted keys, O(log n), no null key
LinkedHashMap: HashMap + doubly-linked list for order, O(1)
LRU cache: LinkedHashMap(cap, 0.75f, true) — removeEldestEntry
TreeMap use cases: range queries, floor/ceiling/subMap operations

question: What is the difference between HashSet and TreeSet and how does a HashSet work?
quickAnswer: HashSet is backed by a HashMap (keys are elements, values are a dummy object) — O(1) add/contains/remove, no duplicates, no order. TreeSet is backed by a TreeMap — O(log n), sorted, no duplicates. HashSet's performance depends on correct hashCode() and equals() implementation.
keyPoints:
HashSet = HashMap where keys are the set elements
O(1) contains: hashCode() → bucket → equals() check
TreeSet = TreeMap — sorted, O(log n) all operations
LinkedHashSet: insertion order, O(1)
Duplication check: equals() + hashCode() must be consistent
Null: HashSet allows one null; TreeSet doesn't (comparison needed)

question: What is the fail-fast behavior of Java collections?
quickAnswer: Most standard collections (ArrayList, HashMap, etc.) are fail-fast: if the collection is structurally modified while iterating (add/remove outside the iterator), a ConcurrentModificationException is thrown. The collection maintains a modCount counter; the iterator checks it each step. To safely modify while iterating, use iterator.remove() or CopyOnWriteArrayList.
keyPoints:
Fail-fast: throws ConcurrentModificationException on concurrent structural modification
ModCount: counter incremented on structural changes; iterator snapshot
Safe removal: use Iterator.remove() during iteration
Fail-safe alternatives: CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap.entrySet()
ConcurrentModificationException can happen in single-thread too (modifying inside for-each)

question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
quickAnswer: Checked exceptions extend Exception (not RuntimeException) — the compiler forces you to handle or declare them (throws). They represent recoverable conditions (IOException, SQLException). Unchecked exceptions extend RuntimeException — no compile-time enforcement. They represent programming errors (NPE, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException) or unrecoverable conditions.
keyPoints:
Checked: extends Exception, must catch or throws — IOException, SQLException
Unchecked: extends RuntimeException — NPE, IllegalArgumentException, ClassCastException
Error: extends Error — OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError — don't catch
Checked = recoverable scenario; Unchecked = programming bug
Modern practice: many frameworks use unchecked to avoid forced catch blocks

question: What happens in a try-catch-finally block? When does finally NOT execute?
quickAnswer: finally always executes after try/catch, whether or not an exception was thrown. It executes even if there's a return in try or catch. It does NOT execute if: System.exit() is called, the JVM crashes, or the thread is killed. If finally also throws an exception, it masks the original exception.
keyPoints:
finally: always runs — even on return, break, continue
System.exit() or JVM crash: skips finally
finally exception: masks original exception from try/catch
Return value: if try returns x and finally returns y, y wins
try-with-resources (Java 7+): automatically calls close() — preferred over finally for resources

question: What is try-with-resources and how does it work?
quickAnswer: try-with-resources (Java 7+) automatically closes resources that implement AutoCloseable. The resource's close() method is called at the end of the try block, even if an exception is thrown. Multiple resources are closed in reverse order. It replaces the try-finally idiom for resource cleanup and properly handles 'suppressed exceptions'.
keyPoints:
Resource must implement AutoCloseable (or Closeable)
Close called automatically in reverse order of declaration
Suppressed exceptions: if close() throws and try throws, close exception is suppressed (accessible via getSuppressed())
Old way: try { ... } finally { rs.close(); } — buggy if close throws
Java 9+: can use effectively-final variables declared outside

question: What is the difference between map() and flatMap() in Java Streams?
quickAnswer: map() transforms each element to one output element — 1-to-1 mapping. flatMap() transforms each element to a Stream of elements and then flattens them — 1-to-many. Use flatMap when each element contains a collection you want to unwrap into the main stream.
keyPoints:
map: Stream<T> → Stream<R>, one element in → one element out
flatMap: Stream<T> → Stream<R>, one element in → zero-to-many elements out (flattened)
Common use: list of lists → flat stream; Optional chaining
flatMap(Collection::stream): flatten a list of lists
Difference from mapToObj: flatMap takes T → Stream<R>
codeExample:
List<List<Integer>> nested = List.of(List.of(1,2), List.of(3,4));
nested.stream()
      .flatMap(Collection::stream)
      .forEach(System.out::println); // 1 2 3 4

question: What is the difference between intermediate and terminal operations in Java Streams?
quickAnswer: Intermediate operations (filter, map, sorted, limit) return a new Stream and are lazy — they don't execute until a terminal operation is reached. Terminal operations (collect, forEach, count, reduce, findFirst) trigger the pipeline and produce a result or side effect. A stream can be consumed by only one terminal operation.
keyPoints:
Lazy evaluation: intermediate ops build a pipeline, no work done yet
Terminal ops: trigger the pipeline, produce a result
Laziness benefit: short-circuit — findFirst stops after first match
Common intermediate: filter, map, flatMap, distinct, sorted, limit, skip
Common terminal: collect, forEach, count, reduce, findFirst, anyMatch, toList
Streams are single-use: terminal op exhausts the stream

question: What are the four main functional interfaces in Java?
quickAnswer: Function<T,R>: takes T, returns R (T → R). Predicate<T>: takes T, returns boolean. Consumer<T>: takes T, returns void (side effect). Supplier<T>: takes nothing, returns T. They all live in java.util.function and are used throughout Streams, Optional, and lambda-based APIs.
keyPoints:
Function<T,R>: apply() — transform — used in map()
Predicate<T>: test() — filter condition — used in filter()
Consumer<T>: accept() — side effect — used in forEach()
Supplier<T>: get() — factory — used in Optional.orElseGet()
BiFunction<T,U,R>: two inputs; BiConsumer, BiPredicate similarly
UnaryOperator<T>: Function<T,T> specialization; BinaryOperator<T>: BiFunction<T,T,T>

question: What is the difference between Comparator.comparing() and natural ordering?
quickAnswer: Natural ordering uses the Comparable interface (compareTo method) — objects define their own order. Comparator provides external ordering without modifying the class. Comparator.comparing(Person::getName) creates a comparator by key extractor. You can chain with .thenComparing() for multi-field sorts and .reversed() to flip order.
keyPoints:
Comparable: natural order, single order per class, compareTo()
Comparator: external order, multiple orderings possible, compare()
Comparator.comparing(keyExtractor): concise lambda-friendly factory
.thenComparing(): chain for secondary sort
.reversed(): flip order
Collections.sort(list, comparator) or list.sort(comparator)

question: What is the difference between Java IO and Java NIO?
quickAnswer: Java IO (java.io) is stream-based, blocking — one thread per connection. Java NIO (java.nio) is buffer/channel-based with a Selector — one thread can handle many connections (non-blocking). NIO is better for high-concurrency servers; IO is simpler for simple file/stream operations. NIO.2 (Java 7) added the modern Path/Files API.
keyPoints:
IO: stream-based, blocking, one thread per connection
NIO: buffer + channel + selector, non-blocking, multiplexing
Channel: bidirectional; Stream: unidirectional
ByteBuffer: direct (native memory) vs heap
Selector: single thread monitors multiple channels for readiness
NIO.2 (Java 7): java.nio.file — Path, Files, WatchService

question: How do you read a file efficiently in Java (modern approach)?
quickAnswer: For small files: Files.readString(path) or Files.readAllLines(path) in one shot. For large files: Files.lines(path) returns a lazy Stream<String> — process line by line without loading all into memory. Always specify charset explicitly. Use try-with-resources for all readers/streams. BufferedReader wrapping FileReader is the classic approach.
keyPoints:
Files.readString(path): entire file as String (Java 11+)
Files.readAllLines(path): all lines as List<String>
Files.lines(path): lazy Stream<String>, good for large files
BufferedReader: classic approach, buffered for efficiency
Always specify Charset: StandardCharsets.UTF_8
try-with-resources: ensures stream/reader is closed

question: What is the difference between a Thread and a Runnable in Java?
quickAnswer: Thread is a class that represents a thread of execution. Runnable is a functional interface (run() method) representing the task. Prefer Runnable/Callable over extending Thread — it separates the task from the execution mechanism, and Java doesn't allow multiple inheritance, so extending Thread blocks other inheritance.
keyPoints:
Thread: heavyweight — one OS thread per Java thread
Runnable: just the task (no return value); Callable: task with return value + exception
Prefer Runnable/Callable with ExecutorService over raw Thread
new Thread(runnable).start() vs executorService.submit(callable)
Virtual threads (Java 21): lightweight threads on carrier threads — 'cheap' Runnable execution

question: What is the volatile keyword in Java?
quickAnswer: volatile ensures that reads and writes to a variable go directly to main memory — not a CPU cache. This guarantees visibility: all threads see the most recent write. However, volatile does NOT guarantee atomicity (i++ on a volatile int is still not thread-safe — it's read-modify-write). Use AtomicInteger for atomic compound operations.
keyPoints:
volatile: visibility guarantee — flushes to main memory on write, reads from main memory
Does NOT guarantee atomicity: i++ is 3 operations (read, add, write)
No memory reordering past volatile read/write (happens-before)
Use case: flags (volatile boolean running = true), double-checked locking
AtomicInteger: CAS-based (compare-and-swap) — volatile + atomic operations

question: What is a deadlock and how do you prevent it?
quickAnswer: Deadlock occurs when two or more threads each hold a lock the other needs — they wait forever. The four Coffman conditions must all hold: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait. Prevention: always acquire locks in the same order (lock ordering), use tryLock with timeout, or avoid nested locks.
keyPoints:
Coffman conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
Prevention: consistent lock ordering across all threads
tryLock(timeout): back off if can't acquire
Detection: thread dumps (jstack) show BLOCKED threads with cycle
Java deadlock example: Thread A locks X then Y; Thread B locks Y then X
Avoidance: minimize lock scope; prefer higher-level concurrent utilities

question: What is the ExecutorService and why should you use it instead of raw threads?
quickAnswer: ExecutorService manages a pool of reusable threads. Benefits: avoids thread creation overhead per task, controls max thread count, provides Future for results, handles exception propagation, and supports clean shutdown. Executors.newFixedThreadPool(n), newCachedThreadPool(), and newSingleThreadExecutor() are common factories.
keyPoints:
Thread pools: reuse threads, avoid creation/destruction cost
submit(Callable): returns Future<T> to get result later
execute(Runnable): fire-and-forget (no result)
shutdown(): graceful (finish queued tasks); shutdownNow(): interrupt running tasks
newCachedThreadPool(): unbounded — risky under load
newFixedThreadPool(n): bounded, predictable resource usage

question: What is autoboxing and unboxing in Java, and what are the performance pitfalls?
quickAnswer: Autoboxing automatically converts primitives to their wrapper class (int → Integer). Unboxing converts back (Integer → int). Pitfall: autoboxing in tight loops creates many short-lived objects, increasing GC pressure. The worst trap is using Long instead of long in a sum loop — can create millions of Long objects.
keyPoints:
Autoboxing: int → Integer, done by compiler via Integer.valueOf()
Integer.valueOf() caches -128 to 127 (Integer cache)
Unboxing null Integer → NullPointerException!
Performance trap: boxing in loops — prefer primitive arrays/streams (IntStream)
Long sum vs long sum: Long version creates objects; long is 10-100x faster

question: Why does Integer == Integer return true for small values but false for large values?
quickAnswer: Integer.valueOf() caches Integer objects for values -128 to 127. Autoboxed integers in this range share cached instances, so == returns true. Outside this range, new objects are created, so == returns false. Always use .equals() to compare Integer values — never rely on == for wrapper types.
keyPoints:
Integer cache: -128 to 127 — Integer.valueOf(127) always returns same object
Outside cache range: new Integer object each time
Integer a = 128; Integer b = 128; a == b → false
This applies to Byte, Short, Integer, Long (-128 to 127), Character (0 to 127)
Boolean: Boolean.TRUE and FALSE are singletons
Always use .equals() for semantic equality of wrappers

question: What is wrong with java.util.Date and Calendar, and how does java.time fix it?
quickAnswer: java.util.Date is mutable, not thread-safe, month indices start at 0 (confusing), and has poor API design. Calendar is clunky and still mutable. java.time (Java 8, inspired by Joda-Time) provides immutable, thread-safe classes: LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime, ZonedDateTime, Instant, Duration, Period.
keyPoints:
Date problems: mutable, not thread-safe, months 0-indexed, confusing setters
Calendar: verbose, still mutable, timezone handling poor
LocalDate/LocalTime/LocalDateTime: no timezone, immutable
ZonedDateTime: full timezone-aware datetime
Instant: UTC epoch timestamp for machine time
Duration: time-based amount; Period: date-based amount

question: What is the difference between LocalDateTime and ZonedDateTime?
quickAnswer: LocalDateTime has no timezone info — it's a calendar date and time like '2024-01-15T10:30'. It means different things in different timezones. ZonedDateTime includes a timezone (e.g., '2024-01-15T10:30+05:30[Asia/Kolkata]') — it represents an unambiguous moment. Use ZonedDateTime for scheduling across timezones; use Instant for machine timestamps.
keyPoints:
LocalDateTime: no timezone — ambiguous across regions
ZonedDateTime: LocalDateTime + ZoneId — specific moment in time
Instant: UTC milliseconds since epoch — use for logging, storage
ZoneId vs ZoneOffset: ZoneId handles DST; ZoneOffset is a fixed offset
Conversion: instant.atZone(ZoneId.of("Asia/Kolkata")) → ZonedDateTime

question: How does Java garbage collection work? What are the different GC algorithms?
quickAnswer: GC reclaims heap memory from objects with no live references. The heap is divided into Young Gen (Eden + Survivors) and Old Gen (Tenured). Most objects die young — Minor GC quickly cleans Eden. Long-lived objects are promoted to Old Gen, cleaned by Major/Full GC. Major GCs are expensive (stop-the-world). JVM offers: Serial, Parallel, G1 (default since Java 9), ZGC, Shenandoah.
keyPoints:
Young Gen (Eden + S0 + S1): Minor GC — fast, frequent
Old Gen: Major GC — slow, infrequent — stop-the-world for Serial/Parallel
G1 GC: region-based, predictable pause time, default in Java 9+
ZGC / Shenandoah: low-latency (<1ms pauses), concurrent marking
GC roots: stack vars, static fields, JNI refs — reachable from these = alive
SoftReference, WeakReference: for caches; cleared by GC under pressure

question: What is the difference between Minor GC and Full GC? Which is more expensive?
quickAnswer: Minor GC collects only the Young Generation (Eden + Survivor spaces) — fast (milliseconds), happens frequently. Full GC collects the entire heap (Young + Old + Metaspace) and compacts memory — slow (seconds), causes stop-the-world pause for most collectors. Frequent Full GCs indicate that objects are living too long or memory is under-provisioned.
keyPoints:
Minor GC: Young Gen only — fast, triggers when Eden full
Full GC: entire heap — slow, stop-the-world (Serial/Parallel/G1)
Promotion failure: triggers Full GC if Old Gen can't accept Young Gen survivors
G1 mixed collection: collects Young + some Old regions to avoid Full GC
Monitor: -verbose:gc or GC logs; high Full GC frequency = problem

topicTitle: Collections Internal Working — HashMap, Comparators & Iterators
question: How does HashMap work internally in Java? How does it handle collisions?
quickAnswer: HashMap uses an array of buckets. put(k,v): computes hashCode(k), applies a spread function, indexes into the array. Collisions (two keys hash to the same bucket) are handled by chaining: a linked list at that bucket. In Java 8+, buckets with 8+ entries convert to a balanced tree (TreeNode) for O(log n) worst case instead of O(n).
keyPoints:
Array of Node[] (buckets), default capacity 16, load factor 0.75
hashCode() → spread (XOR with shifted high bits) → index = hash & (n-1)
Collision: chaining via linked list at bucket
Java 8 treeification: linked list → red-black tree when bucket size ≥ 8
Resize (rehash): when size > capacity * loadFactor → new array double size, re-insert all
null key: always bucket 0

topicTitle: Collections Internal Working — HashMap, Comparators & Iterators
question: What is the contract between hashCode() and equals() and why must it be respected?
quickAnswer: The contract: if a.equals(b) is true, then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode() must be true. The reverse is NOT required (hash collisions are allowed). Breaking this contract means equal objects may be stored in different buckets, making HashMap/HashSet unable to find them — get() returns null for keys that 'exist'.
keyPoints:
equals → same hashCode (required)
same hashCode does NOT imply equals (collision is fine)
Breaking contract: HashMap.get() won't find 'equal' key in different bucket
Common mistake: override equals without hashCode (IDE will warn)
Lombok @EqualsAndHashCode, Objects.hash() for convenience
If hashCode uses mutable fields: changing the field after putting in map loses the entry

question: What is the Java Reflection API and what can it do?
quickAnswer: Reflection allows inspecting and manipulating classes, methods, fields, and constructors at runtime, regardless of access modifiers. It can: instantiate classes by name, invoke private methods, read private fields, get annotations, and dynamically proxy interfaces. Used heavily in frameworks (Spring, JUnit, Hibernate) but is slow and breaks encapsulation — avoid in normal application code.
keyPoints:
Class.forName(): load class by string name
getDeclaredMethod/Field(): access private members
setAccessible(true): bypasses access checks
Invocation cost: ~10-100x slower than direct calls
Security: SecurityManager (deprecated Java 17+) could restrict
Use cases: DI frameworks, ORM, test frameworks, serialization libraries

question: What are Java annotations and what are retention policies?
quickAnswer: Annotations are metadata markers on code (@Override, @Autowired). Retention policy controls when annotation info is available: SOURCE (discarded after compile — @Override, @SuppressWarnings), CLASS (in .class file, not at runtime — default), RUNTIME (visible via reflection at runtime — @Autowired, @Entity). Most framework annotations need RUNTIME retention.
keyPoints:
RetentionPolicy.SOURCE: compile-time only (javac reads, then discards)
RetentionPolicy.CLASS: .class file, NOT available at runtime (default)
RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME: available via reflection — needed for frameworks
@Target: restricts where annotation can be used (TYPE, METHOD, FIELD, etc.)
Annotation processing (APT): generate code at compile time (@Lombok)

question: What is Java serialization and what are its problems?
quickAnswer: Serialization converts an object to a byte stream (for storage/network). Implement Serializable (marker interface) and Java does the rest. Problems: performance overhead, brittle across class changes (serialVersionUID must match), security risks (deserialization gadget chains can execute arbitrary code), and creates tight coupling. Prefer JSON/Protobuf/Avro in modern systems.
keyPoints:
Serializable: marker interface — ObjectOutputStream/ObjectInputStream handle it
serialVersionUID: class version — mismatch causes InvalidClassException
transient: exclude fields from serialization
Security risk: deserialization of untrusted data → remote code execution
Externalizable: write/readExternal() for custom control
Modern alternatives: Jackson (JSON), Protobuf, Kryo, Avro

question: What is the difference between Serializable and Externalizable?
quickAnswer: Serializable is a marker interface — Java handles serialization automatically using reflection. Externalizable provides full control: you implement writeExternal() and readExternal() manually. Externalizable is faster (no reflection), uses less space, but requires more code. With Externalizable, you must also provide a no-arg constructor.
keyPoints:
Serializable: automatic (reflection-based), easy, slower, bigger
Externalizable: manual (writeExternal/readExternal), faster, more code, smaller output
Externalizable needs public no-arg constructor (used during deserialization)
Customization with Serializable: writeObject/readObject callbacks
Externalizable: complete control — choose which fields and format

question: What is Optional in Java and when should you use it?
quickAnswer: Optional<T> is a container that may or may not hold a non-null value — it explicitly models the possibility of absence. It prevents NPE by forcing callers to handle the absent case. Use it as return type for methods that may have no result. Don't use it as method parameter, in collections/fields, or in constructors.
keyPoints:
Optional.empty(): no value; Optional.of(x): non-null value; Optional.ofNullable(x): nullable
orElse(default): get value or default
orElseGet(supplier): lazy default (preferred for expensive defaults)
orElseThrow(): throw if empty — asserting presence
map/flatMap: transform if present
Don't: use in collections (List<Optional<T>>), as fields, as method params

question: When should you use parallelStream() and what are its pitfalls?
quickAnswer: parallelStream() splits work across the ForkJoinPool common pool. Use when: the operation is CPU-bound, the data set is large (>10K elements), the operation per element is expensive, and the reduction is associative/commutative. Pitfalls: thread safety of shared state, incorrect results from non-associative operations, high overhead for small data, I/O-bound work shouldn't be parallel.
keyPoints:
Uses ForkJoinPool.commonPool() — shared across the JVM
Good for: CPU-bound, large data, no shared mutable state
Bad for: small data (overhead > gain), I/O, non-associative reduce, shared state
Order: parallelStream + sorted = expensive (forced sequential merge)
Custom pool: submit(() -> stream.parallel()...) inside ForkJoinPool
Never mutate shared collections inside parallel operations

question: What is the enhanced for-loop (for-each) and when can't you use it?
quickAnswer: The enhanced for-loop (for (Type x : collection)) iterates over any Iterable or array without managing an index. It's cleaner and less error-prone. You can't use it when: you need the index, you need to modify the collection while iterating, you need to traverse two collections simultaneously, or you need to iterate in reverse.
keyPoints:
for-each works on: arrays and anything implementing Iterable<T>
Can't modify collection (ConcurrentModificationException)
No index access — use traditional for-loop when index needed
Can't reverse iterate or skip elements
Under the hood: calls iterator() on Iterable; for arrays uses index counter

question: What is the ternary operator and how is it different from an if-else?
quickAnswer: The ternary operator (condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse) is an expression — it produces a value and can be used inline in assignments, method arguments, or return statements. if-else is a statement — it controls flow but doesn't produce a value. Ternary is ideal for simple conditional assignments; if-else is better for multi-statement branches.
keyPoints:
Ternary is an expression (produces a value); if-else is a statement
Can nest ternary but deeply nested ternaries hurt readability
Both branches must be type-compatible (or promotable)
Useful in: assignments, method arguments, string building
Performance: identical to if-else; no difference after compilation
codeExample:
String label = score >= 50 ? "Pass" : "Fail";
System.out.println("Status: " + (active ? "Active" : "Inactive"));

question: What is covariant return type in Java?
quickAnswer: Covariant return type (Java 5+) allows an overriding method to return a subtype of the parent method's return type. For example, if Animal.clone() returns Animal, Dog.clone() can return Dog. This removes the need for casting at the call site and makes the API more type-safe.
keyPoints:
Overriding method can declare return type = original type OR a subtype
Avoids casting: Dog d = dog.clone() instead of (Dog) animal.clone()
Type safety: compiler guarantees the narrower type
Covariance = return type narrows; contravariance = parameter type widens (not native in Java)
Arrays are covariant: Dog[] can be assigned to Animal[] — but this is a runtime trap (ArrayStoreException)

question: What is the instanceof operator and how has it improved in modern Java?
quickAnswer: instanceof checks if an object is an instance of a type at runtime. Classic usage requires a separate cast after the check. Java 16+ pattern matching for instanceof combines the check and cast: if (obj instanceof String s) gives you the typed variable s directly — no cast needed. This eliminates a class of ClassCastException risks.

question: What are default methods in interfaces and why were they introduced?
quickAnswer: Default methods (Java 8) allow interfaces to provide a concrete method implementation. They were introduced to allow the Java Collections API to be extended (e.g., adding forEach, stream(), removeIf to Collection) without breaking the millions of existing implementations. A class can override a default method. If two interfaces declare conflicting defaults, the implementing class must override to resolve the conflict.
keyPoints:
Declared with 'default' keyword; implementing classes inherit it automatically
Purpose: backward-compatible interface evolution (Java 8 Streams retrofit)
Diamond conflict: two interfaces with same default → compile error → class must override
Class always wins: if a superclass and interface provide the same method, class version wins
Default ≠ abstract: it has a body; classes don't have to override it
Static methods in interfaces (Java 8): utility methods, not inherited

question: What is the difference between final, finally, and finalize in Java?
quickAnswer: final is a modifier: on a variable = constant (can't reassign), on a method = can't be overridden, on a class = can't be subclassed. finally is a try-catch block section that always runs for resource cleanup. finalize() is a deprecated Object method called by GC before collecting — unreliable (never guaranteed to run) and replaced by Cleaners/AutoCloseable.
keyPoints:
final variable: reference can't change (object's internals still mutable)
final method: prevents override — used for security/template methods
final class: no subclassing — String, Integer are final
finally: cleanup block in try — always runs except on System.exit()
finalize(): called by GC before collection — deprecated (Java 9+), unreliable
Use AutoCloseable + try-with-resources instead of finalize

question: What is a bounded type parameter in Java generics and how do you use it?
quickAnswer: A bounded type parameter restricts which types can be used as a generic argument. <T extends Number> means T must be Number or a subclass — you can call Number's methods on T. Multiple bounds are allowed: <T extends Comparable<T> & Serializable>. This provides both flexibility and type safety beyond raw types.
keyPoints:
<T extends Type>: T must be Type or its subtype — upper bound
Multiple bounds: <T extends A & B & C> — class first, then interfaces
Can call bound's methods on T inside the method
Comparable bound: <T extends Comparable<T>> enables sorting algorithms
Recursive bound: <T extends Comparable<T>> — T must be comparable with itself
Difference from wildcard: T is a named type parameter; ? is a wildcard (no name binding)

question: What is the difference between a byte stream and a character stream in Java IO?
quickAnswer: Byte streams (InputStream/OutputStream) work with raw bytes — use for binary data (images, audio, serialized objects). Character streams (Reader/Writer) add encoding/decoding — they work with characters using a specified charset (default: platform encoding). Always use character streams for text files and specify the charset explicitly (StandardCharsets.UTF_8) to avoid platform-dependent behavior.
keyPoints:
Byte streams: InputStream/OutputStream — raw binary, no encoding
Character streams: Reader/Writer — encode/decode chars using charset
InputStreamReader: bridges byte → char stream (specify charset!)
FileReader/FileWriter: convenient but use platform default charset (avoid)
Best practice: new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(is, StandardCharsets.UTF_8))
Buffering: BufferedReader/BufferedWriter reduce system calls significantly

question: How do you parse and format dates in Java using DateTimeFormatter?
quickAnswer: DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("dd-MM-yyyy") creates a formatter. Use .parse(str) on the date class to parse, or .format(formatter) on the date object to format. DateTimeFormatter is immutable and thread-safe — share as a constant. Common predefined formatters: ISO_LOCAL_DATE (2024-01-15), ISO_DATE_TIME. Locale matters for month names.
keyPoints:
DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern(): custom pattern — M=month, d=day, y=year, H=24hr, h=12hr
Parsing: LocalDate.parse("15-01-2024", formatter)
Formatting: date.format(formatter)
Thread-safe: safe to use as static final constant
Predefined: ISO_LOCAL_DATE, ISO_LOCAL_DATE_TIME, ISO_INSTANT
Locale-specific: ofPattern("d MMMM", Locale.ENGLISH) for "15 January"

question: What are SoftReference, WeakReference, and PhantomReference in Java?
quickAnswer: Java has four reference strengths. Strong: normal reference — never GC'd while reachable. Soft: cleared when JVM needs memory (good for caches). Weak: cleared at next GC cycle — WeakHashMap uses this so keys are GC'd when no strong refs exist. Phantom: enqueued after the object is finalized — used for cleanup actions (replacement for finalize).
keyPoints:
Strong reference: never collected while any strong ref exists
SoftReference: GC clears only under memory pressure — cache-friendly
WeakReference: GC clears at next cycle — WeakHashMap, ThreadLocal keys
PhantomReference: object already finalized — used with ReferenceQueue for cleanup
ReferenceQueue: JVM enqueues refs when their referent is collected
WeakHashMap: map entries automatically removed when key is GC'd

topicTitle: Collections Internal Working — HashMap, Comparators & Iterators
question: What is the difference between ConcurrentHashMap and a synchronized HashMap?
quickAnswer: Collections.synchronizedMap(map) wraps a HashMap — every read and write locks the entire map, allowing only one thread at a time. ConcurrentHashMap (Java 5+) uses segment-based locking (Java 7) or lock-striping + CAS (Java 8+) — reads are lock-free, and only the affected bucket is locked on write. ConcurrentHashMap offers much higher throughput under concurrent access.
keyPoints:
synchronizedMap: coarse-grained single lock — all operations serialized
ConcurrentHashMap: fine-grained locking — reads are lock-free (volatile reads)
Java 8 CHM: CAS for insert, synchronized on single bucket node for collision
Bulk operations: forEach, reduce, search (parallelism threshold configurable)
No null keys/values in ConcurrentHashMap (would be ambiguous)
Prefer CHM for concurrent workloads; synchronizedMap for simple wrapping of existing map

question: What is a dynamic proxy in Java and how does it work?
quickAnswer: A JDK dynamic proxy creates an implementation of one or more interfaces at runtime. You provide an InvocationHandler whose invoke() method intercepts every interface method call. Frameworks like Spring AOP and MyBatis use this to add cross-cutting concerns (logging, transactions) without modifying the original class. It only works for interfaces — for classes, use CGLIB or ByteBuddy.
keyPoints:
Proxy.newProxyInstance(classLoader, interfaces[], handler): creates proxy at runtime
InvocationHandler.invoke(proxy, method, args): intercepts every call
JDK proxy: interface-only; CGLIB/ByteBuddy: class proxying (subclasses)
Spring @Transactional: backed by proxy — method called from within same class bypasses it!
Performance: slight overhead per call vs direct method call
Reflection inside handler: method.invoke(target, args) to delegate to real object

question: What is serialVersionUID and what happens if you don't declare it?
quickAnswer: serialVersionUID is a version identifier for a Serializable class. During deserialization, Java checks that the serialVersionUID in the stream matches the class. If you don't declare it, Java auto-generates one based on the class structure — any change to the class (adding a field, method) regenerates it, breaking deserialization of old data. Always declare it explicitly to control compatibility.
keyPoints:
Auto-generated: based on class name, fields, methods — fragile
Declare explicitly: private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;
Mismatch: InvalidClassException at deserialization time
Changing serialVersionUID intentionally: signals intentional incompatibility
Common gotcha: IDE refactoring (renaming a method) changes auto UID
Not needed for Externalizable (you handle the format yourself)

question: What are enums in Java and how are they different from int constants?
quickAnswer: An enum is a type-safe set of named constants. Unlike int constants (public static final int STATUS_ACTIVE = 1), enums prevent invalid values at compile time — you can't pass 99 where a Status is expected. Enums can have fields, methods, and implement interfaces. Each constant is a singleton instance of the enum class.
keyPoints:
Type safety: enum enforces valid values at compile time
Enum is a class: can have fields, constructors, methods
Each constant: singleton instance of the enum type
values(): returns all constants as array; valueOf("NAME"): lookup by name
ordinal(): position (avoid using — fragile); name(): string name
Switch with enum: compiler can warn about missing cases

question: Can an enum implement an interface or extend a class?
quickAnswer: Enums can implement interfaces — this allows polymorphism with enum constants. Enums cannot extend classes because all enums implicitly extend java.lang.Enum<T>, and Java doesn't support multiple class inheritance. Implementing interfaces on enums is a common pattern for strategy-like behavior (each constant implements the interface differently).
keyPoints:
Enum implicitly extends java.lang.Enum<E> — no more class extension possible
Enum CAN implement interfaces — common pattern for per-constant behavior
Abstract method in enum: each constant must provide its own implementation
Enums are final implicitly — can't subclass an enum
Enum constants are public static final — thread-safe singletons

question: How do EnumSet and EnumMap differ from regular Set and Map?
quickAnswer: EnumSet is backed by a long bitmask — all operations are O(1) and extremely fast. EnumMap stores values in an array indexed by enum ordinal — also O(1) and more memory-efficient than HashMap. Both require the key type to be an enum. They are the recommended collections when keys/elements are enums.
keyPoints:
EnumSet: bitmask-backed, O(1) all ops, no boxing, no null
EnumMap: array-backed (indexed by ordinal), O(1), preserves enum declaration order
EnumSet.of(A, B, C): concise; EnumSet.allOf(Day.class): all values
Faster and smaller than HashSet<Enum>/HashMap<Enum,V>
Thread-safety: neither is thread-safe — wrap with Collections.synchronized*
Common use: permission sets (EnumSet<Permission>), config maps

question: How can you look up an enum constant safely without throwing an exception?
quickAnswer: Enum.valueOf("NAME") throws IllegalArgumentException for unknown names. To look up safely, iterate values() with a try-catch, or pre-build a Map<String, MyEnum> from all values(). A static fromCode() method pattern is common when the lookup key is a field (not the name). Avoid using ordinal() for lookup — it's fragile when constants are reordered.
keyPoints:
valueOf(name): throws IllegalArgumentException if name not found
Safe pattern: pre-built Map<String, MyEnum> in a static block
ordinal(): fragile — reordering constants changes values
Annotation or field: store display name/code in a field; add fromCode() factory
Stream approach: Arrays.stream(values()).filter(v -> v.code.equals(code)).findFirst()
Common bug: valueOf() is case-sensitive

question: What is the Singleton pattern and how do you implement it thread-safely?
quickAnswer: Singleton ensures a class has only one instance. Thread-safe options: (1) Eager initialization — create at class load time using a static final field. (2) Bill Pugh — static inner class holder (lazy + thread-safe without synchronization). (3) Enum singleton — JVM guarantees one instance, handles serialization safely. Avoid double-checked locking unless the field is volatile.
keyPoints:
Eager: static final instance = new Singleton() — simple, always created
Lazy (Bill Pugh): static inner holder class — loaded on first access, JVM ensures thread safety
Enum: best for serialization safety — JVM prevents creating extra instances
Double-checked locking: needs volatile on instance field (Java 5+)
Singleton pitfalls: hard to test (mock difficult), hides dependencies
Spring @Component is a singleton by default within the ApplicationContext

question: What is the Builder pattern and when should you use it?
quickAnswer: Builder separates object construction from its representation, letting you build complex objects step by step. Use it when: an object has many optional parameters (avoids telescoping constructors), the object must be immutable (no setters), or construction order matters. Lombok's @Builder auto-generates the builder. Unlike constructors with many params, Builder calls are self-documenting.
keyPoints:
Fluent API: Person.builder().name("Alice").age(30).build()
Solves telescoping constructor problem (constructors with 5+ params)
Enables immutability: all fields set in builder, object has no setters
Director class (GoF): optional — directs the build steps
Lombok @Builder: generates the builder class automatically
Validation: add checks in build() before constructing the object
codeExample:
Person person = Person.builder()
    .name("Alice")
    .age(30)
    .email("alice@example.com")
    .build();

question: What is the difference between Strategy and Template Method patterns?
quickAnswer: Both define algorithms, but differ in how variation is achieved. Template Method uses inheritance: a base class defines the algorithm skeleton with abstract steps; subclasses fill in the steps. Strategy uses composition: the algorithm is extracted to a separate interface, and the client holds a reference to a strategy object. Prefer Strategy (composition over inheritance) when the algorithm should be swappable at runtime.
keyPoints:
Template Method: inheritance — base class has the flow, subclass fills in steps
Strategy: composition — algorithm interface injected at runtime
Template Method: fixed algorithm structure, variable steps
Strategy: algorithm entirely swappable; object holds a reference to it
Java examples: Collections.sort() = Strategy (Comparator); InputStream.read() = Template Method
Prefer Strategy for testability and runtime flexibility

question: What is the Observer pattern and how is it used in Java?
quickAnswer: Observer defines a one-to-many dependency: when a Subject changes state, all registered Observers are notified automatically. In Java, this is the basis for event listeners (ActionListener in Swing), PropertyChangeListener, and reactive streams. The classic java.util.Observable is deprecated; prefer custom EventBus, guava EventBus, or Spring ApplicationEvent.
keyPoints:
Subject (Observable): maintains list of observers; notifies on state change
Observer: interface with update() callback method
Loose coupling: subject doesn't know observer types
Java built-in: PropertyChangeSupport + PropertyChangeListener
Spring: ApplicationEventPublisher + @EventListener
Pitfall: order of notification not guaranteed; memory leak if observers not removed

question: What is CompletableFuture and how is it different from Future?
quickAnswer: Future (Java 5) represents an async result but has a blocking get() — you can't chain operations or add callbacks. CompletableFuture (Java 8) adds: non-blocking callbacks (thenApply, thenAccept), chaining pipelines, combining multiple futures (allOf, anyOf), exception handling (exceptionally, handle), and explicit completion (complete(), completeExceptionally()). It makes async programming composable.
keyPoints:
Future.get(): blocking — wastes a thread waiting
CompletableFuture: callback-based — register what to do when done
thenApply(fn): transform result (like Stream.map)
thenAccept(consumer): consume result (no return)
thenCompose(fn): chain — fn returns another CompletableFuture (flatMap equivalent)
combineWith/allOf: wait for multiple futures
Default executor: ForkJoinPool.commonPool() (unless Async variant with custom executor)
codeExample:
CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchUser(id))
    .thenApply(user -> enrichWithProfile(user))
    .thenAccept(profile -> cache.put(id, profile))
    .exceptionally(ex -> { log.error("Failed", ex); return null; });

question: What is the difference between thenApply, thenCompose, and thenCombine?
quickAnswer: thenApply(fn) transforms the result synchronously — like map(). thenCompose(fn) chains another async operation — fn returns a CompletableFuture — like flatMap(), avoids nesting. thenCombine(other, fn) waits for two independent futures and combines their results. Use thenCompose when your callback itself returns a future; use thenApply for simple transformations.
keyPoints:
thenApply: T → U (sync transform, like map)
thenCompose: T → CompletableFuture<U> (async chain, like flatMap — avoids CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<U>>)
thenCombine: (T, U) → V — two independent futures merged
thenAcceptBoth: like thenCombine but no return value
thenRun: no input, no output — just a side effect after completion
Async variants (thenApplyAsync): run callback on a different thread

question: How do you handle exceptions in a CompletableFuture pipeline?
quickAnswer: Two main methods: exceptionally(fn) — handles error, provides a fallback value (like catch); handle(fn) — runs on both success and failure, receives (result, exception) and always produces a value (like finally). whenComplete is similar to handle but doesn't transform the result. If no exception handler is added, exceptions are swallowed until get() is called.
keyPoints:
exceptionally(fn): catches exception, returns fallback — like a catch block
handle(fn): runs always — receives (T result, Throwable ex), one will be null
whenComplete(consumer): side-effect on both paths, doesn't change result
Without a handler: exceptions propagate silently to get() as ExecutionException
ExceptionHandler chain: each stage can add its own handler
Nested futures: exceptions in thenCompose propagate to the outer future

question: How do you run multiple CompletableFutures in parallel and wait for all of them?
quickAnswer: CompletableFuture.allOf(cf1, cf2, cf3) returns a CompletableFuture<Void> that completes when all complete. To collect results, join each future after allOf completes. anyOf(cf1, cf2, cf3) returns a future that completes as soon as any one completes. For a list of futures, use allOf(list.toArray(new CompletableFuture[0])).
keyPoints:
allOf: waits for ALL — returns Void, results must be collected separately
anyOf: completes on FIRST — returns Object (result of first completed)
After allOf.join(): safe to call cf.join() on each future (already done)
List of futures: allOf(futures.toArray(new CompletableFuture[0]))
Stream pattern: futures.stream().map(CompletableFuture::join) — collects results
Timeout: future.orTimeout(3, TimeUnit.SECONDS) (Java 9+)

question: What is the difference between synchronized and ReentrantLock?
quickAnswer: synchronized is a keyword — simple, automatic unlock on exception/return, but inflexible. ReentrantLock is a class providing: tryLock() with timeout, lockInterruptibly() (can be interrupted while waiting), fairness option (longest-waiting thread gets lock first), and multiple Condition objects. ReentrantLock must be explicitly unlocked — always in a finally block.
keyPoints:
synchronized: automatic unlock, no timeout, not interruptible, one implicit condition
ReentrantLock: explicit lock/unlock, tryLock(timeout), lockInterruptibly(), multiple Conditions
Fairness: new ReentrantLock(true) — FIFO order (slower but prevents starvation)
Reentrant: same thread can lock multiple times; must unlock same number of times
Condition: lock.newCondition() → await()/signal()/signalAll() (replaces wait/notify)
Always unlock in finally: lock.lock(); try { ... } finally { lock.unlock(); }

question: What is ReadWriteLock and when should you use it?
quickAnswer: ReadWriteLock (ReentrantReadWriteLock) maintains two locks: a read lock (shared — multiple threads can hold it simultaneously) and a write lock (exclusive — only one thread at a time, blocks all readers). Use when reads are frequent and writes are rare — allows high read concurrency without sacrificing write safety. Typical use: caches, configuration objects.
keyPoints:
Read lock: multiple threads can acquire concurrently (shared)
Write lock: exclusive — no readers or writers allowed concurrently
Use case: read-heavy, write-rare workloads (e.g., in-memory cache)
Downgrading: write → read lock is allowed (acquire read, release write)
Upgrading: read → write lock is NOT supported (deadlock risk)
Starvation risk: with heavy read traffic, writers may be starved

question: What are CountDownLatch, CyclicBarrier, and Semaphore? When do you use each?
quickAnswer: CountDownLatch: one or more threads wait until N other threads finish (one-shot, not reusable). CyclicBarrier: N threads all wait at a barrier point before proceeding together (reusable — 'cyclic'). Semaphore: limits concurrent access to a resource — like a pool of N permits. Semaphore(1) is a binary semaphore (mutex). CountDownLatch for one-time startup sync; CyclicBarrier for iterative phased work; Semaphore for rate limiting.
keyPoints:
CountDownLatch: await() blocks until countdown reaches 0; countDown() from workers
CyclicBarrier: await() blocks until all N parties arrive; then all proceed together
Semaphore: acquire() takes a permit; release() returns it; blocks if no permits available
CountDownLatch: NOT reusable — use Phaser for repeated countdowns
CyclicBarrier: reusable — resets after each cycle; optional Runnable at barrier
Semaphore use: connection pool, rate limiter, bounding concurrent requests

question: What is the happens-before relationship in Java memory model?
quickAnswer: The Java Memory Model (JMM) guarantees that actions in one thread are visible to another only if there is a happens-before (HB) edge. Key HB relationships: program order within a thread; monitor unlock HB monitor lock of the same monitor; volatile write HB volatile read of the same variable; Thread.start() HB all actions in the started thread; thread termination HB Thread.join() return.
keyPoints:
Without HB: compiler/CPU may reorder instructions — stale caches possible
Volatile write HB volatile read: guarantees visibility across threads
synchronized release HB synchronized acquire: all writes before unlock visible after lock
Thread.start() HB thread body: thread sees all actions before start()
Thread.join() HB caller: caller sees all actions of the joined thread
Atomic operations (CAS): also establish HB

question: What is JDBC and what are the main steps to query a database?
quickAnswer: JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is the standard API for connecting Java applications to relational databases. Main steps: (1) Load driver (auto-loaded via SPI in modern JDBC). (2) Get a Connection from DriverManager or DataSource. (3) Create a Statement or PreparedStatement. (4) Execute query. (5) Process ResultSet. (6) Close resources (use try-with-resources).
keyPoints:
DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass): creates a connection
PreparedStatement: preferred over Statement — prevents SQL injection, reusable
ResultSet: cursor over rows — rs.next() advances; rs.getString("col") gets value
Connection is expensive: always close; use a connection pool (HikariCP)
try-with-resources: auto-closes Connection, PreparedStatement, ResultSet
JDBC URL format: jdbc:mysql://host:port/db?params

question: What is the difference between Statement, PreparedStatement, and CallableStatement?
quickAnswer: Statement executes ad-hoc SQL — risk of SQL injection. PreparedStatement precompiles SQL with ? placeholders — prevents injection, better performance for repeated queries (parsed once). CallableStatement executes stored procedures. Always prefer PreparedStatement for queries with user input. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
keyPoints:
Statement: no parameters, vulnerable to SQL injection, parsed each time
PreparedStatement: compiled once, ? placeholders, safe from injection
CallableStatement: calls stored procedures {call proc_name(?)}
Performance: PreparedStatement caches query plan — faster for repeated execution
SQL injection: Statement + string concat = vulnerability; PreparedStatement = safe
Batch inserts: ps.addBatch(); ps.executeBatch() — far faster than individual inserts

question: What is a connection pool and why is it critical in production JDBC applications?
quickAnswer: Creating a database connection is expensive (TCP handshake, auth, protocol negotiation). A connection pool pre-creates and maintains a set of reusable connections. Application code borrows a connection and returns it after use — the underlying connection stays open. HikariCP is the fastest and most popular pool (default in Spring Boot). Without pooling, each request could take 50–200ms just to open a connection.
keyPoints:
Connection creation cost: ~50–200ms — too slow per request
Pool: pre-creates N connections; app borrows and returns
HikariCP: Spring Boot default; ~130μs to borrow a connection
Key settings: maximumPoolSize, connectionTimeout, idleTimeout, maxLifetime
Leak detection: HikariCP can log unclosed connections (leakDetectionThreshold)
DataSource interface: pool exposes DataSource — code is pool-agnostic
codeExample:
// application.properties (Spring Boot + HikariCP)
# spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=10
# spring.datasource.hikari.connection-timeout=3000
# spring.datasource.hikari.idle-timeout=600000
